1: kerof'i leYisrael nigla avon Efraim veraot Shomron: 'When I would have healed Israel, the iniquity of Ephraim was exposed.' Rapha (heal) is the covenantal restoration verb — YHWH as healer of the covenant people's sins is developed throughout the prophets (Jer 3:22; 17:14; 33:6; Isa 53:5). The divine healing intention is frustrated not by divine incapacity but by the exposure of sin: nigla (was revealed, laid bare). The metaphor is clinical: YHWH approaches to heal; the wound's true nature is exposed; the patient refuses treatment. Samaria's sin is not private — it is exposed in the public record of 'theft and banditry within' (beqirbo, within it/within the house). The domestic and civic corruption are as visible to YHWH as if laid on an examining table.
2: velo yomeru lilevavam kol ra'atam zakarti: 'But they do not consider in their hearts that I remember all their evil.' Zakar (remember) functions in the OT as judicial memory — YHWH's remembering is not passive recall but active prosecution. The community's self-delusion is the failure to apply zakar to themselves: they do not reckon with the fact that YHWH's memory is comprehensive and will produce judgment. Their deeds surround them (savavum) like witnesses at a trial; they are 'before my face' (neged panai). The forensic imagery — memory, deeds as witnesses, before the face — frames Israel's conduct as a legal case already prepared against them.
3: bemaltam yesamechu melekh uvechacheshan sarim: 'They make the king happy with their wickedness, and win the princes with their treachery.' Machale (make happy/rejoice) and kachash (treachery, lying) describe court politics: the royal court is sustained by a system of flattering treachery. Those who succeed in the royal court do so by corrupting the court with wickedness. The system of mutual congratulation in sin (rulers congratulating subjects; subjects congratulating rulers) is the social face of covenant breakdown — when leaders reward wickedness rather than righteousness, the covenant order inverts.
4: kullam mena'afim kivir bo'er me'ofeh: 'They are all adulterers, burning like an oven that the baker does not need to stoke.' The oven metaphor (tannur) runs through vv. 4-7 — an oven that burns so intensely it requires no stoking through the night, then erupts by morning. Mena'afim (adulterers) is used in Hosea both literally (sexual unfaithfulness) and theologically (covenant infidelity with Baal). The self-sustaining, unstoked oven describes the autonomous nature of their sin: it does not need external kindling because it has become self-perpetuating. The baker who can sleep while the oven sustains itself = YHWH observing how Israel's sin has achieved its own momentum.
5: yom malkeinu hechelim sarim chemat minayin: 'On the day of our king, the princes made themselves sick with wine.' Royal festival days in the ancient Near East involved prescribed feasting; here the feast becomes a scene of royal debauchery in which the princes corrupt the king himself (mashach yado et lotze'im, the king stretches out his hand with scoffers). The king who should model covenant faithfulness becomes instead a fellow traveler with those who mock. The royal feast that should celebrate YHWH's provision becomes a scene of covenant mockery.
6: ki qirevu chetannur libbam beorvam: 'For their hearts smolder like an oven as they scheme; their baker sleeps through the night, but in the morning it blazes like a flaming fire.' The conspiracy (orvam, their plotting/lurking) is the thing that keeps the oven of the heart hot through the night of apparent inactivity. While the baker (= the king? the people's patron?) sleeps, the plotters' hearts burn. By morning, the conspiracy erupts into open action. The image captures how political and religious betrayal gestate quietly and then express themselves suddenly — the visible sin has long-prepared invisible roots.
7: kullam yichammu katannur veyochelu et-shoftehem: 'They are all as hot as an oven, and they devour their rulers; all their kings have fallen. None of them calls on me.' The political instability of the northern kingdom's final decades (assassinations, rapid turnover of kings) is framed as the logical consequence of the burning conspiracy: those who burn hot enough devour their own leaders. 'None of them calls on me' (ein qore elai mehem) is the theological summary: in the midst of political chaos, no one performs the only action that could address the underlying cause. The religious failure (no calling on YHWH) and the political failure (devouring rulers) are the same crisis at different levels.
8: Efraim baamim hu yitbolal Efraim hayah ugah bilti hafukah: 'Ephraim mixes himself among the peoples; Ephraim is a flat cake that has not been turned over.' The ugah (round flat bread) that has not been turned (bilti hafukah) is burned on one side, raw on the other — useless for sustenance. As a description of Ephraim, it means: partially assimilated into foreign cultures, partially still Israelite, but incomplete and unusable in either direction. The mixing (hitbolal) with peoples is the opposite of the covenant distinction that set Israel apart. The metaphor is domestically precise: a half-baked loaf that ruins both the baking and the eating.
9: achlu zarim kocho vehu lo yeda gam seivah zerukah bo vehu lo yeda: 'Foreigners have consumed his strength, and he does not know it. Gray hairs are scattered on him, yet he does not know it.' The pathos of 7:9 is the ignorance of decline: Ephraim does not recognize that his vitality is being sapped. Kocho (his strength) is being consumed by foreigners even as he continues in his political and religious routines. The gray hairs are the visible sign of advancing age and weakness that the person himself does not perceive. This is the prophetic diagnosis: the most dangerous condition is not crisis but unrecognized decline, when the patient feels fine while dying.
10: veyigne ga'on Yisrael befanav: 'The pride of Israel testifies against him to his face, yet they do not return to the LORD.' Ga'on Yisrael (the pride of Israel) is used in Hosea 5:5 and here: their very pride acts as witness against them. They will not return (lo shavu) to YHWH despite the self-evident testimony of their own failure. Teshuvah (return/repentance) is Hosea's central call — the verb shuv appears repeatedly throughout the book as the prescribed response to the covenant crisis. Its negation here ('they do not return') is the book's central diagnosis: the medicine is available but refused.
11: vayehi Efraim keyonah pote't ein lev: 'Ephraim is like a senseless dove, lacking all judgment.' The dove (yonah) flutters between Egypt and Assyria, calling one direction then the other, with no settled policy. Pote't (simple, senseless, easily deceived) describes the bird's inability to evaluate threats — it flies toward danger as readily as safety. Israel's foreign policy of alternating between Egyptian and Assyrian alliances is diagnosed as political senselessness masquerading as strategic flexibility. The dove lacks the wisdom (lev, heart = understanding) to see that neither great power can provide what YHWH alone can give.
12: ka'asher yelchu efros aleihem reshti: 'As they go, I will cast my net over them; I will bring them down like the birds of the sky.' The hunting net (reshet) catches the senseless dove of v11 — the very aimless fluttering that the dove does leads it into the net. YHWH's judgment is described as the inevitable consequence of their own trajectory: they fly toward judgment. Yissireim keshema'at ledatam (I will discipline them as their congregation has heard) — the announced covenant curses are now enacted; they are not surprises.
13: oy lahem ki nadedu mimeni shod lahem ki pasha'u vi: 'Woe to them, for they have strayed from me! Destruction to them, because they have rebelled against me!' Oy (woe) is the lament-cry that precedes judgment announcements throughout the prophets. Two verbs describe the sin: nadad (stray, wander away, be driven) and pasha (rebel, transgress, breach the covenant relationship). The first suggests drifting; the second suggests active defiance. 'I would redeem them [efdem], yet they speak lies against me' — YHWH's intention to redeem is present even in the woe-oracle; the obstacle is their lying, not his willingness. Padah (redeem/ransom) is the exodus-redemption word: YHWH came to do for them what he did in Egypt, but they rejected it.
14: velo za'aqu elai belibbam ki yeylellu al mishkevotam: 'They do not cry to me from their hearts; they merely howl on their beds.' The distinction between genuine prayer and ritual howling is one of Hosea's sharpest observations. Za'aq (cry out) from the lev (heart) = authentic petition; yeylellu (they howl/wail) on their beds is emotional expression without theological engagement. They howl for grain and wine — physical provision — but do not address the covenant God who alone can give. 'They gash themselves' (yitgoredu) — practices associated with Baal worship (1 Kgs 18:28). Their religious activity is Baal-inflected even in distress.
15: va'ani yasarti chizaqti zero'otam va'elay yachashvu ra: 'I trained and strengthened their arms, yet they devise evil against me.' Yasar (train, discipline, instruct) combined with chizaq (strengthen) — YHWH's investment in forming Israel as a nation capable of covenant life. The arms he strengthened are now directed against him in evil plotting (yachashvu ra). This is the covenant ingratitude that Hosea makes concrete: the very capacities YHWH developed in Israel are used to plan evil against him. It echoes the Deuteronomic warning that prosperity would lead to forgetting YHWH (Deut 8:11-18) — the trained arms forget their trainer.
16: yashuvun lo al-al hayah kequeshet remiyah: 'They return, but not to the Most High; they are like a treacherous bow.' Lo al-al (not to the Most High) — they return to something, but not to YHWH. The qeshet remiyah (treacherous bow, deceitful bow) is the bow that fails when you draw it — either not flexible enough or too flexible, so it shoots inaccurately. Israel is a weapon that cannot be relied upon, always veering away from the intended target. The same image appears in Ps 78:57: 'they were turned aside like a treacherous bow.' Their princes fall by the sword; their tongue's arrogance brings 'mockery in Egypt' (laeg beEretz Mitzraim) — the very nation they ran to for help witnesses their humiliation.
